% =============================================================================
% DRAFT 2026-05-04 -- pivote ENSO. REPLACEMENT TEXT for paper1_climate_projections.Rmd
% line ~839 (Discussion paragraph on jack mackerel non-identification).
%
% Tres versiones paralelas (A/B/C) según el ratio sigma_post/sigma_prior del
% rho_enso[3] del refit Stan. Felipe pega solo la versión que aplique.
%
% Placeholders LIVE -- iguales a los de appendix_E_enso_section.Rmd:
%   -0.022    -0.81    0.80
%   0.979      0.490
%
% También hay una propuesta de UPDATE para appendix_spatial_jurel.Rmd §E.5
% "Implication for the main claim" al final de este archivo (line 374 actual).
% =============================================================================

% -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
% UBICACION: paper1/paper1_climate_projections.Rmd, line ~839
% Reemplaza el párrafo que actualmente comienza con
% "The fact that the jack mackerel shifter is not identified at the Centro-Sur
%  scale is itself a substantive result rather than a nuisance, but its policy
%  reading depends on a careful distinction between non-identification and
%  no climate effect."
% =============================================================================

%%%% VERSION A -- ENSO IDENTIFICA (ratio <= 0.7) %%%%

\noindent\textit{[Versión A -- usar si el ratio del lag-1 principal $\le 0.70$:]}
The non-identification of jack mackerel productivity in response to
local Centro-Sur SST and chlorophyll anomalies (Section
\ref{identification}) admits a basin-scale resolution. Appendix
\ref{appendix-spatial} reports a refit of the state-space
specification in which jack mackerel productivity is forced by the
ENSO Niño 3.4 index in place of the Centro-Sur coastal shifters.
The basin-scale elasticity is identified at
$\rho_{\text{jur}}^{ENSO} = -0.022$ with
$90\%$ credible interval
$[-0.81,\,0.80]$ and
posterior-to-prior standard-deviation ratio of
$0.979$, comfortably inside the threshold for
material identification. The reading consistent with this result is
that the climate sensitivity of jack mackerel at the Centro-Sur scale
is real but operates through teleconnection forcing of the equatorial
Pacific rather than through the upwelling-band SST and chlorophyll
anomalies that resolve anchoveta and sardine---a reading anticipated
by @Arcos2001-jq and @Pena-Torres2017-gn on
qualitative grounds and now sharpened to a point estimate. The
distinction between non-identification on coastal shifters and basin-scale
identification matters for policy: it implies that adaptive management
for jack mackerel under climate change must respond to ENSO indicators
rather than to local oceanographic monitoring, and it relocates the
relevant spatial unit of intervention from the EEZ to the regional
SPRFMO management framework.

%%%% VERSION B -- ENSO NO IDENTIFICA (ratio > 0.85) %%%%

\noindent\textit{[Versión B -- usar si el ratio del lag-1 principal $> 0.85$:]}
The non-identification of $(\rho_{\text{jur}}^{SST},
\rho_{\text{jur}}^{CHL})$ at the Centro-Sur scale is itself a
substantive result rather than a nuisance, but its policy reading
depends on a careful distinction between \emph{non-identification} and
\emph{no climate effect}. The two are conflated only by reduced-form
specifications that force a coefficient onto every regressor regardless
of identification. Appendix \ref{appendix-spatial} reports four
converging tests of this distinction, all consistent with the absence
of an identifiable shifter for jack mackerel within the
$2000$--$2024$ Centro-Sur biomass record: (i) varying the spatial
aggregation of the coastal shifters across three nested domains
$\mathcal{D}_1 \subset \mathcal{D}_2 \subset \mathcal{D}_3$ leaves the
posterior-to-prior ratios in $0.998$--$1.014$; (ii) augmenting the
biomass record with a Northern Chilean acoustic series under a common
elasticity moves the ratio only to $0.94$--$0.99$; (iii) replacing the
coastal shifters with the basin-scale ENSO Niño 3.4 index returns a
ratio of $0.979$, also above the threshold for
material identification; and (iv) an attempt to incorporate the
SPRFMO regional assessment series fails on coherence grounds. Existing
empirical evidence on jack mackerel documents that climate does affect
the species at scales broader than local coastal anomalies---@Arcos2001-jq
on intrusion of the $15\,^{\circ}$C isotherm during the
$1997$--$98$ El Niño, @Pena-Torres2017-gn on ENSO-driven shifts in
location choices in the Chilean fishery---but each of these documents climatic effects on
\emph{margins} (location choice, range distribution, spawning timing,
phenological response) that are not necessarily captured in the
elasticity of structural productivity at an annual aggregate. The
convergence of four independent tests on this null at the Centro-Sur
scale points to a structural rather than aggregation-driven
limitation of the available record: closing the identification gap
will require either a longer biomass record or a basin-scale
assessment with explicit cross-stock spillover, not a different choice
of spatial aggregation, biomass source, or basin-scale shifter within
the present record. We accordingly hold $r^{*}_{\text{jurel}}$ fixed
in the projections of Tables \ref{tab:growth_compstat} and
\ref{tab:trip_compstat}, and report the resulting fleet-level
elasticities under that constraint.

%%%% VERSION C -- MARGINAL (0.70 < ratio <= 0.85) %%%%

\noindent\textit{[Versión C -- usar si el ratio del lag-1 cae en
$(0.70, 0.85]$, identificación marginal:]}
The non-identification of jack mackerel productivity on local Centro-Sur
shifters (Section \ref{identification}) admits a basin-scale test that
returns partial movement of the posterior. Appendix
\ref{appendix-spatial} reports a state-space refit in which jack
mackerel productivity is forced by the ENSO Niño 3.4 index, returning
$\rho_{\text{jur}}^{ENSO} = -0.022$ with $90\%$
credible interval
$[-0.81,\,0.80]$ and
posterior-to-prior ratio of $0.979$. The point estimate
is consistent in direction with the qualitative literature on
ENSO-mediated effects on jack mackerel
(@Arcos2001-jq; @Pena-Torres2017-gn) but sits between
the thresholds for material identification ($0.70$) and clear
non-identification ($0.85$): the magnitude is suggestive but not
sharply pinned on the available $24$-year sample. We report the
projections of $r^{*}_{\text{jurel}}$ in Tables
\ref{tab:growth_compstat} and \ref{tab:trip_compstat} both
under the basin-scale ENSO posterior and under the conventional
fixed-$r^{*}$ benchmark, and flag the unresolved magnitude as a
substantive limitation that a longer biomass record or a complementary
basin-scale assessment would need to close.


% =============================================================================
% UBICACION: paper1/sections/appendix_spatial_jurel.Rmd, §E.5 "Implication for
% the main claim" (line 342). Replacement text para integrar la 4ta evidencia.
% =============================================================================

% Reemplaza el párrafo en line 344-365 que comienza con
% "The main-text claim that the climate sensitivity of jack mackerel
%  cannot be resolved from the available 2000-2024 Centro-Sur biomass record
%  is robust across three complementary tests of spatial scale and sample
%  structure."

%%%% VERSION B/C (NULO o MARGINAL) %%%%

The main-text claim that the climate sensitivity of jack mackerel
cannot be sharply resolved from the available $2000$--$2024$
Centro-Sur biomass record is robust across four complementary tests of
spatial scale, sample structure, and forcing modality. First, the
spatial robustness refit of Sections \ref{sec:appendix-spatial-spec}
and \ref{sec:appendix-spatial-results} varies the environmental
aggregation across $\mathcal{D}_1$, $\mathcal{D}_2$, and
$\mathcal{D}_3$ while holding the biomass record fixed: the
posterior-to-prior ratios remain in $0.998$--$1.014$ across all three
domains. Second, the dual-source extension of Section
\ref{sec:appendix-spatial-dual} adds a second biomass series from the
Northern Chilean acoustic record under a common climate elasticity,
exploiting the $0.88$ log-scale correlation between the two Chilean
series: the ratios fall only to $0.94$--$0.99$. Third, the basin-scale
test of Section \ref{sec:appendix-spatial-enso} replaces the local
shifters with the ENSO Niño 3.4 index under a deliberately tighter
prior $\mathcal{N}(0, 0.5)$, returning a posterior-to-prior ratio of
$0.979$. Fourth, an attempt to incorporate the
SPRFMO regional assessment series fails on coherence grounds,
indicating that the broader geographic aggregate is not integrable
with the Centro-Sur record under a single shifter. The convergence of
these four tests indicates a genuinely flat identification of the
shifter for jack mackerel within the Centro-Sur biological domain,
not an artefact of insufficient spatial extent, insufficient sample
size, mis-specified forcing scale, or poor source aggregation.

%%%% VERSION A (ENSO IDENTIFICA) %%%%

\noindent\textit{[Versión A -- alternativa si ENSO identifica.]}
The main-text non-identification of jack mackerel productivity on
local Centro-Sur shifters admits a basin-scale resolution. Three
spatial-extension tests rule out the conjecture that simply enlarging
the spatial aggregation would suffice: under $\mathcal{D}_1$,
$\mathcal{D}_2$, and $\mathcal{D}_3$ the posterior-to-prior ratios
remain in $0.998$--$1.014$, and the dual-source extension adding the
Northern Chilean acoustic record reduces them only to $0.94$--$0.99$.
The basin-scale test of Section \ref{sec:appendix-spatial-enso},
however, replaces the local SST and chlorophyll shifters with the ENSO
Niño 3.4 index and identifies the elasticity at
$-0.022$ with posterior-to-prior ratio
$0.979$. The diagnostic reading of the appendix is
therefore that jack mackerel productivity at the Centro-Sur scale
\emph{is} climatically coupled, but the relevant spatial scale of the
forcing is the equatorial Pacific rather than the local upwelling
band---a result anticipated by the qualitative biological literature
and now embedded in the structural projections of Section
\ref{projections}. The auxiliary attempt to incorporate the SPRFMO
regional assessment series fails on coherence grounds and is reported
only as a methodological note in the Discussion.
